evals.prompt: We've got a production bug. Users who registered before June 2024 can't reset their password — they click the reset link in the email and get a blank white page. Users who registered after that date are fine. Started getting reports about this on Monday, seems to have been broken for a while without us noticing. Can you write this up as a ticket?
evals.expected_output: A Bug ticket with Expected (what the reset flow should do) and Actual (blank page for pre-June-2024 users) sections. Should not prescribe a fix — just document the discrepancy.
assertions: Spec is classified as a Bug
Spec has an Expected section describing correct system behaviour
Spec has an Actual section describing the broken behaviour
Expected and Actual sections are clearly distinct — they describe different things
Spec does not prescribe a fix or implementation approach
The affected cohort (users registered before June 2024) is captured
Spec title is outcome-focused or describes the failure, not a solution
Entire spec is under 40 lines of Markdown

evals.prompt: We keep getting timeouts on the checkout endpoint during peak traffic. The p99 latency spikes to 8s but we don't know why — could be a slow DB query, an external payment API, or something in our own business logic. We need to investigate before we can fix anything. Can you make a ticket for this?
evals.expected_output: A Spike ticket framing the investigation as a question to answer, with concrete deliverables (a finding and recommendation), not a feature or fix. Should be timeboxed research.
assertions: Spec is classified as a Spike
Spike description frames the work as a question to investigate, not a task to complete
Spec includes at least two concrete deliverables the spike should produce
Spec does not prescribe the answer or solution upfront
Context section explains why this investigation is needed now
Spec does not prescribe specific technologies or tools for the investigation
Spec title includes 'Spike' or is otherwise clearly research-oriented
Entire spec is under 40 lines of Markdown

evals.prompt: Our CI pipeline takes 18 minutes to run. The main culprit is the integration test suite — it runs sequentially and doesn't use any parallelism. We know what needs doing: split the test suite into shards and run them in parallel across multiple runners. This is an engineering task, no user-facing change. Can you write it up?
evals.expected_output: A Task ticket (not a Story — no direct end-user value) with a clear task description and verifiable acceptance criteria. The primary beneficiary is the engineering team.
assertions: Spec is classified as a Task
Task description clearly states what needs to be done
Acceptance criteria are verifiable and measurable
Spec does not prescribe specific CI tools or runner infrastructure
Context section explains why this work matters (pipeline speed impact)
Acceptance criteria focus on outcomes, not implementation steps
Spec does not classify as a Story — the work has no direct end-user benefit
Entire spec is under 40 lines of Markdown
